passages.title: Earth's Seven Natural Wonders
passages.body:
Long ago, people made a list of the most amazing places on Earth and called them the Seven Natural Wonders. These places remind us how powerful and beautiful nature can be.

The list includes the Grand Canyon in the United States, Mount Everest in Asia, the Great Barrier Reef in Australia, the Northern Lights in the far north, Paricutin Volcano in Mexico, the Harbor of Rio de Janeiro in Brazil, and Victoria Falls in Africa. Each one was chosen because it is huge, rare, or breathtaking.

For example, Mount Everest is the tallest mountain in the world. Climbers train for years before they try to reach its summit. The Great Barrier Reef is so large that astronauts can see it from space, and it is home to thousands of kinds of fish. The Northern Lights paint the night sky with green and pink waves of light.

The Seven Natural Wonders show that our planet is full of surprises. They invite us to explore, to protect, and to feel small in the best way.

[Map: locations of the Seven Natural Wonders] [Photo caption: The Great Barrier Reef from above.]

questions.q: What is the central idea of this text?
choices: Mount Everest is the tallest mountain.
The Seven Natural Wonders show how amazing Earth is.
The Great Barrier Reef has many fish.
Astronauts can see Earth from space.

questions.q: Which detail SUPPORTS the idea that the wonders are huge or rare?
choices: People made a list long ago.
Climbers train for years to reach Everest's summit.

questions.q: What does the [Map: locations of the Seven Natural Wonders] feature help readers do?
choices: See where the wonders are around the world

questions.q: Which sentence is the BEST one-sentence summary?
choices: Earth has seven famous natural places that show how powerful nature is.
The Northern Lights are green and pink.
Astronauts saw the reef from space.

questions.q: What is the topic of the text?
choices: The Seven Natural Wonders of Earth

passages.title: Down to the Deepest Place
passages.body:
First, scientists wanted to know what was at the bottom of the ocean. They knew the deepest spot was the Mariana Trench, but no one had ever been there. Next, they had a problem: the water near the bottom pushes down with the weight of an elephant on every fingertip. A normal submarine would be crushed.

So, engineers built a special submersible with very thick walls and tiny windows. They tested it carefully. Then, in 1960, two explorers climbed inside and rode it almost seven miles down into the trench. The trip took five hours.

When they finally landed on the bottom, they were surprised. They expected the trench to be empty. Instead, they saw a small fish swim past their window. Even in the darkest, coldest place on Earth, life had found a way.

Finally, what they learned changed science forever. Today, autonomous robots also visit the trench to take pictures and bring back samples, so we can keep learning about this amazing place.

questions.q: How is this text organized?
Which signal words tell you about the text structure?
What problem did the engineers have to solve?
choices: The trench was too far away.
The water pressure would crush a normal submarine.

questions.q: Which sentence is the BEST summary?
choices: A fish lives in the trench.
Scientists built a special submersible to reach the deepest place on Earth and discovered life there.

questions.q: What happened AFTER the explorers landed on the bottom?
choices: They saw a fish swim by.

passages.title: Weird and Wondrous Rocks
passages.body:
Rocks may look quiet and boring, but some rocks are as strange as anything in a story book. The Wave, in Arizona, swirls across the desert like ribbons of orange candy. Giant's Causeway, in Ireland, is a row of stone columns that fit together as neatly as honeycomb. People long ago thought a giant must have built it.

In Norway, a huge rock called Kjeragbolten is wedged between two cliffs, sitting in the air like a frozen marble. Brave hikers stand on top of it, while the wind whispers around them.

How did these wonders form? Patient nature is the artist. Wind, water, and ice slowly shape the stone for millions of years. A river can carve a canyon as easily as a knife slices cake — if you give it long enough.

The next time you pick up a plain pebble, remember: every rock has a long, secret story.

questions.q: Which sentence uses a SIMILE?
choices: Rocks may look quiet and boring.
The Wave swirls across the desert like ribbons of orange candy.
Brave hikers stand on top of it.

questions.q: "The wind whispered around them" is an example of —
What does the author mean by "Patient nature is the artist"?
choices: A real artist painted the rocks.
Nature slowly shapes the rocks like an artist makes art.

questions.q: What is the central idea of this text?
choices: Some rocks around the world are amazing, and nature shaped them slowly.

questions.q: Which detail SUPPORTS the idea that rocks take a long time to form?
choices: Wind, water, and ice slowly shape stone for millions of years.

passages.title: Maya and the Grand Canyon
passages.body:
Maya stared down into the Grand Canyon and her stomach flipped. The trail looked like a tiny brown thread along the cliff. "I can't do this," she whispered. Her older brother, Leo, just smiled. "Yes you can. One step at a time."

The first hour was the hardest. Maya's legs shook, and twice she sat down and almost cried. Each time, Leo waited. He didn't laugh, and he didn't tell her to hurry. He pointed at lizards on the rocks and at hawks circling far below. Slowly, Maya started to look around instead of straight down.

By lunchtime, she had walked farther than she ever had in her life. By afternoon, she was the one pointing things out to Leo. When they finally reached the bottom and stood beside the wide green river, Maya laughed out loud.

That night, in her sleeping bag, she thought about the trail. She had been so afraid at the top. But the canyon hadn't gotten smaller — she had gotten braver. From now on, when something looked too big to do, she would remember: one step at a time.

questions.q: What is the THEME of this story?
choices: Big problems can be solved one small step at a time.
Always bring lunch on a hike.

questions.q: How does Maya CHANGE from the beginning to the end?
choices: She becomes braver and more confident.
She gets tired and gives up.

questions.q: Which is the BEST summary of the story?
choices: Maya saw a lizard and a hawk.
Maya was afraid to hike the Grand Canyon, but with her brother's patient help she made it to the bottom and felt proud.
The river at the bottom was green.
Leo did not laugh at his sister.

questions.q: What is the central message the author wants us to remember?
choices: The canyon hadn't gotten smaller — she had gotten braver.

questions.q: What did Leo do that helped Maya the most?
choices: He waited patiently and pointed out interesting things.
He told her to go faster.
He left her alone on the trail.
